scenario: Asking the price of fruit at a stall

transcript.text: Bom dia! Quanto custa o quilo da manga?
Bom dia! Tá a seis reais o quilo, tudo fresquinho.
Essa aqui já tá no ponto?
Tá sim, pode levar essa que ela é boa.
Então me dá um quilo, por favor.

translation.text: Good morning! How much is the mango per kilo?
Good morning! It's six reais a kilo, all nice and fresh.
Is this one ripe already?
It is, you can take this one, it's good.
Then give me a kilo, please.

grammarNotes: 'Tá' is the everyday spoken contraction of 'está' — extremely common at a feira and in casual speech generally.

scenario: Buying in bulk for a better price

transcript.text: Vou levar bastante tomate hoje. Leva mais barato se eu levar mais?
Levando três quilos, eu faço a cinco reais o quilo.
Fechado! E cebola, tem também?
Tenho. Quer um maço de couve pra levar também? Tá bem barata.
Pode ser, coloca um maço aí.

translation.text: I'm going to buy a lot of tomatoes today. Is it cheaper if I take more?
If you take three kilos, I'll do five reais a kilo.
Deal! And onions, do you have those too?
I do. Want a bunch of collard greens too? It's really cheap.
Sure, throw in a bunch.

grammarNotes: 'Fechado!' (literally 'closed!') is how Brazilians casually confirm a deal — equivalent to 'deal!' or 'you got it!'.

scenario: A little friendly haggling over the price

transcript.text: Duas dúzias de laranja, quanto fica?
Fica vinte reais.
Nossa, faz um precinho? Sou freguesa toda semana!
Já que é freguesa, fica dezoito.
Show, obrigada!

translation.text: Two dozen oranges, how much does that come to?
That comes to twenty reais.
Wow, can you give me a little discount? I'm a regular every week!
Since you're a regular, it'll be eighteen.
Great, thank you!

grammarNotes: Haggling at a feira is usually this mild — a small, friendly ask rather than a real back-and-forth negotiation. See the culture notes for more.
slangRegisterNotes: 'Show' here is informal slang for 'great/awesome', very common in casual Brazilian speech.

scenario: Asking a vendor what's fresh and in season

transcript.text: Oi! O que tá fresco hoje?
Hoje o morango e a uva chegaram lindos, colhidos ontem.
E a melancia, tá boa?
Tá doce, pode confiar. Quer que eu escolha uma pra você?
Quero sim, pode caprichar!

translation.text: Hi! What's fresh today?
Today the strawberries and grapes came in beautiful, picked yesterday.
And the watermelon, is it good?
It's sweet, trust me. Want me to pick one for you?
Yes please, pick me your best one!

scenario: Asking for a bag and wrapping

transcript.text: Pode colocar numa sacolinha, por favor?
Claro. Quer que eu embrulhe a alface separado, pra não amassar o resto?
Quero, boa ideia.
Prontinho, pode conferir.

translation.text: Can you put it in a bag, please?
Sure. Want me to wrap the lettuce separately, so it doesn't crush the rest?
Yes, good idea.
All set, feel free to check.

scenario: Deciding how to pay: cash, card, or Pix

transcript.text: Vocês passam cartão ou só dinheiro?
Cartão eu não tenho maquininha ainda, mas faço Pix.
Ah, perfeito, posso fazer um Pix pra você.
Isso, o QR code tá aqui na barraca.
Tá pronto, já caiu?
Caiu sim, obrigada!

translation.text: Do you take card, or only cash?
I don't have a card machine yet, but I do Pix.
Ah, perfect, I can send you a Pix.
That's right, the QR code is here at the stall.
It's done, has it come through?
It has, thank you!

grammarNotes: 'Caiu?' ('did it fall/land?') is the natural way Brazilians ask whether a Pix transfer has arrived — a small but very common idiom around this payment method.

scenario: Paying with cash and a warm goodbye to a regular vendor

transcript.text: Deu quanto no total?
Deu vinte e três reais.
Só tenho uma nota de cinquenta. Tem troco?
Tenho sim, calma que eu já vejo aqui.
Valeu, Seu Beto. Até semana que vem!
Até semana que vem, freguesa! Um abraço.

translation.text: How much did it come to in total?
It came to twenty-three reais.
I only have a fifty note. Do you have change?
I do, hang on, let me check here.
Thanks, Seu Beto. See you next week!
See you next week, my regular! Take care.

grammarNotes: 'Seu' before a first name (Seu Beto) is a warm, informal term of respect for an older or familiar man — common between neighbors and regular customers, not a formal title.
